senses.definition: A round body inside a cell's nucleus where ribosomes are made.
examples: The nucleolus is active during ribosome production.
Researchers studied the nucleolus to understand protein synthesis.
collocations: ribosomal RNA genes in the nucleolus

senses.definition: The breaking down or dissolution of the nucleolus, or, in medical contexts, the breakdown of cell nucleus material.
examples: Nucleolysis can be observed during certain cellular stress responses.
The procedure induced nucleolysis to study nucleolar components.

senses.definition: A term presumably referring to a very small nucleosome-related structure; not widely recognized in standard biology references.
examples: Nucleomicrosome is mentioned in some niche texts with variable definitions.
Further study is needed to confirm what a nucleomicrosome precisely denotes.

senses.definition: A proton or neutron that is part of an atomic nucleus.
examples: The nucleus is made of nucleons.
Nucleons are bound together by the strong force.

senses.definition: A rarely used or misspelled form related to nucleus terms; not standard in common dictionaries.
examples: Some texts list nucleone, though it is not commonly defined.
Be cautious of possible spelling variants like nucleone.

senses.definition: Relating to or consisting of nucleons; used in physics contexts.
examples: Nucleonic properties affect radioactive decay.
Nucleonic interactions are studied in nuclear physics.

senses.definition: In a way related to nucleons; used in highly technical language.
examples: The particle behaved nucleonically under the experiment.
Data were analyzed nucleonically to examine nucleus structure.

senses.definition: Tending to donate an electron pair in chemical reactions; capable of acting as a nucleophile.
examples: A nucleophilic atom attacks the electrophile.
Ammonia is a common nucleophilic reagent.

senses.definition: In a nucleophilic manner; in chemistry contexts describing electron donation behavior.
examples: The molecule attacked nucleophilically.
The reagent behaved nucleophilically under the catalyst.

senses.definition: The qualities of substances that determine how strongly they donate electron pairs in chemical reactions.
examples: Nucleophilicities vary with solvent and temperature.
The study compared nucleophilicities of different reagents.

senses.definition: The tendency of a molecule to donate an electron pair in a chemical reaction.
examples: Nucleophilicity increases with a stronger electron pair.
Solvent can influence nucleophilicity.

senses.definition: The liquid inside the nucleus of a cell, excluding the nucleolus, where much genetic material is housed.
examples: Nucleoplasm contains chromatin before it is organized into chromosomes.
Changes in nucleoplasm can affect gene expression.

senses.definition: Relating to the nucleoplasm; used in technical biology descriptions.
examples: Nucleoplasmatic structures were observed under the microscope.
The term nucleoplasmatic is used in some scholarly articles.

senses.definition: Relating to the nucleoplasm; found in scientific writing.
examples: Nucleoplasmic streaming occurs in some cells.
Researchers studied nucleoplasmic proteins.

senses.definition: Plural of nucleoplasm; regions inside the nucleus that contain the nucleoplasm.
examples: Nucleoplasms were stained to study chromatin.
Different nucleoplasms were observed in the cell samples.

senses.definition: A protein bound to nucleic acid, especially DNA or RNA, within the cell nucleus.
examples: Nucleoproteins help organize genetic material.
The study examined nucleoproteins in chromatin.

senses.definition: Plural of nucleoprotein; proteins bound to nucleic acids within the nucleus.
examples: Nucleoproteins play a role in gene regulation.
Various nucleoproteins were identified in the sample.

senses.definition: A misspelling or variant of nucleoside; a nucleotide component lacking one phosphate group.
examples: In some texts, nucleosid appears as a variant of nucleoside.
Correct terminology uses nucleoside instead of nucleosid.

senses.definition: An enzyme that hydrolyzes nucleosides; used in biochemical contexts.
examples: Nucleosidase activity was measured in the assay.
Inhibitors of nucleosidase were tested.

senses.definition: A molecule formed when a sugar attaches to a nucleobase; the building block of nucleotides in RNA and DNA.
examples: Nucleosides combine with phosphate to form nucleotides.
The nucleoside inosine is important in some RNA editing processes.
